How quickly men forget the most tremendous judgments and return to their former crimes! Though the devastations of the flood were before their eyes, though they came from the lineage of righteous Noah, even during his lifetime wickedness increases greatly. Nothing but the sanctifying grace of the Holy Spirit can remove the sinful desires of the human will and the corruption of the human heart. God’s purpose was that mankind should form many nations and populate all lands. In defiance of God's will and against the counsel of Noah, most people united to build a city and a tower to prevent their separation. Idolatry began, and Babel became one of its main centers. They made one another more daring and determined. Let us learn to encourage one another to love and good works, as sinners stir up and encourage one another to wicked works.

Here is an expression as people speak: The Lord came down to see the city. God is just and fair in everything he does against sin and sinners, and condemns no one without hearing their case. Pious Eber is not found among this ungodly group; for he and his family are called the children of God; their souls did not join themselves to the assembly of these children of men. God allowed them to continue for a time, so that the works of their hands, from which they promised themselves lasting honor, might become their lasting shame. God has wise and holy purposes in allowing the enemies of his glory to carry on their wicked plans for a long time and prosper greatly. Observe the wisdom and mercy of God in the ways used to defeat this undertaking. And the mercy of God in not making the punishment equal to the offense; for he does not deal with us according to our sins. The wisdom of God, in choosing a sure way to stop these proceedings. If they could not understand one another, they could not help one another; this would take them away from their building. God has various means, and effective ones, to frustrate and defeat the projects of proud men who set themselves against him, especially by dividing them among themselves. Despite their unity and stubbornness, God was above them; for whoever hardens his heart against him prospers? Their language was confused. We all suffer from it to this day: in all the pains and trouble used to learn the languages we need, we suffer for the rebellion of our ancestors at Babel. Indeed, those unhappy disputes, which are strifes of words, and arise from misunderstanding one another's words, as far as we know, are due to this confusion of tongues. They stopped building the city. The confusion of their tongues not only unfitted them for helping one another, but they saw the hand of the Lord against them. It is wise to stop what we see God fighting against. God is able to destroy and bring to nothing all the devices and designs of Babel builders; there is no wisdom nor counsel against the Lord. The builders departed according to their families, and the language they spoke, to the countries and places allotted to them. The children of men have never come together again, nor ever will, until the great day when the Son of man shall sit upon the throne of his glory, and all nations shall be gathered before him.

Here is a genealogy, or list of names, ending in Abram, the friend of God, and thus leading toward Christ, the promised Seed, who was the son of Abram. Nothing is left upon record but their names and ages; the Holy Spirit seems to hurry through them to the history of Abram. How little do we know of those that have gone before us in this world, even of those that lived in the same places where we live, as we likewise know little of those who now live in distant places! We have enough to do to mind our own work. When the earth began to be populated, men's lives began to shorten; this was Providence’s wise arrangement.

Here begins the story of Abram, whose name is famous in both Testaments. Even the children of Eber had become worshippers of false gods. Those who are through grace, heirs of the land of promise, ought to remember what was the land of their birth; what was their corrupt and sinful state by nature. Abram’s brethren were Nahor, out of whose family Isaac and Jacob obtained their wives; and Haran, the father of Lot, who died before his father. Children cannot be sure that they will outlive their parents. Haran died in Ur, before the happy removal of the family out of that idolatrous country. It concerns us to hasten out of our natural state, lest death surprise us in it. We here read of Abram’s departure out of Ur of the Chaldees, with his father Terah, his nephew Lot, and the rest of his family, in obedience to the call of God. This chapter leaves them about mid-way between Ur and Canaan, where they dwelt until Terah's death. Many reach Charran, and yet fall short of Canaan; they are not far from the kingdom of God, and yet never come there.
